topic: code review rant — junior dev copy-pasting from ChatGPT

turns.content: i just reviewed a PR that was so clearly copy-pasted from chatgpt that i could practically smell the temperature parameter. how do you even give feedback on that without being a dick about it

Ah, the AI-generated PR. You can usually tell by the suspiciously perfect variable names, comments that explain what the code does rather than why, and that weird ChatGPT tendency to add a `try-catch` around everything.

For feedback without being a dick:

1. **Don't call out the source.** Saying "did you write this or did ChatGPT" is confrontational and doesn't help. Focus on the code, not where it came from.
2. **Ask questions that require understanding.** "Can you walk me through why you chose this approach?" or "What happens if X is null here?" If they can't answer, that's the teaching moment.
3. **Point out the specific problems**, same as any review. "This doesn't handle the edge case where..." or "This pattern doesn't fit our codebase conventions."

The real issue isn't that they used AI — it's that they didn't understand what they submitted. That's the conversation to have.

genau. and like, the code technically works? that's the annoying part. it compiles, passes the basic tests. but it's completely wrong for our architecture. they used an in-memory cache where we explicitly use redis, introduced a dependency we've never used and don't want, and the error handling is this generic catch-all that would swallow every exception into the void

Yeah, that's the classic AI-generated code trap — it produces *plausible* code that satisfies the literal requirement while being completely unaware of your system's context. ChatGPT doesn't know your architecture decisions, your dependency policy, or your error handling philosophy. It writes generic code for a generic codebase.

For the review, I'd structure comments around three buckets:

**Architecture mismatch:**
"We use Redis for caching in this service — see [link to existing pattern]. Can you refactor this to use our existing cache layer?"

**Unapproved dependency:**
"This introduces [library]. We haven't vetted this for production use. Can you check with the team before adding new dependencies? Here's how we currently solve this..."

**Error handling:**
"This catch-all will silently swallow errors we need to know about. We want specific exception types caught with proper logging. Look at [example file] for our pattern."

Keep it instructive, link to existing code. They need to understand the *system*, not just the ticket.

the linking to existing code idea is smart. i think part of the problem is we don't have great onboarding docs for the codebase architecture. which is my fault honestly because i keep saying i'll write them and then never do. classic

It's always the senior devs who know the architecture, say they'll document it, and never do. The knowledge lives in your head and in code review comments scattered across 200 merged PRs.

Pragmatic approach instead of writing The Grand Architecture Document that never gets finished:

1. **ADRs (Architecture Decision Records)** — short markdown files, one per decision. "Why we use Redis instead of in-memory caching." "Why we don't catch generic exceptions." Takes 10 minutes each.
2. **Code review templates** — a checklist in your PR template: "Does this follow our caching pattern? Does it introduce new dependencies? Does it handle errors per our conventions?" Forces the reviewer AND the author to check.
3. **README per service** — just the basics: what it does, key dependencies, important patterns.

You don't need a wiki. You need a handful of ADRs and a PR template. That's 2-3 hours of work total and it prevents this exact situation.

adrs are actually something i've been meaning to set up. the barrier is always that writing docs feels like work that doesn't compile. my brain categorizes it as overhead even though i know it saves time long term. developer brain disease

Developer brain disease is real. If it doesn't produce a binary or pass a test, the dopamine system refuses to engage.

Trick to get past it: write the first ADR *right now* as part of this code review. You're already articulating the reasoning in your review comments — "we use Redis because X, we don't catch generic exceptions because Y." Copy those comments, reformat slightly, commit as an ADR. You've already done the thinking. You're just persisting it.

Format is dead simple:

```
# ADR-001: Caching Strategy
**Status:** Accepted
**Context:** [Why this came up]
**Decision:** [What we chose]
**Consequences:** [Trade-offs]
```

That's it. No novel. If it takes more than 10 minutes, you're overcomplicating it.

okay that's actually a clever reframe. i'm literally writing the reasoning in the PR comments anyway, i just need to put it somewhere persistent instead of letting it rot in a closed PR. fine, you've convinced me. i'll do the first one tonight

The first one is the hardest because you're also setting up the directory structure and figuring out the format. After that it's 5-minute copy-paste jobs. Put them in a `/docs/adr/` folder in the repo so they're version-controlled and discoverable.

And for the junior dev — once you have even 3-4 ADRs, you can link to them in reviews instead of re-explaining. "See ADR-001" is way faster than writing a paragraph about your caching philosophy every time.

yeah. and maybe it'll help them understand the system well enough that when they do use chatgpt — because let's be real they're going to keep using it — at least they can prompt it with the right context. "use redis not in-memory" is a pretty basic constraint to feed it

Exactly. The problem was never "junior dev used AI" — it's "junior dev used AI with zero context about the system." If they feed it your ADRs and existing patterns, the output would be dramatically better.

Some teams even maintain a project-specific AI prompt file or a conventions doc: "When generating code for this service, follow these rules..." It's pragmatic. The tool isn't going away, so you might as well help people use it well rather than pretending they won't.
a conventions file that you can paste into chatgpt. that's actually kind of genius and also kind of dystopian. "please read the ARCHITECTURE.md before prompting your robot to write code for us"

Welcome to 2025. The onboarding doc is now half for humans and half for their LLM of choice. Dystopian? Maybe. Effective? Probably.

But honestly, a good ARCHITECTURE.md helps humans just as much. Whether a junior dev reads it themselves or feeds it to ChatGPT, the outcome is better code. The medium of understanding matters less than whether understanding exists at all.

fair point. okay the real question: how passive-aggressive is too passive-aggressive for this PR review? because right now i have a comment that says "interesting dependency choice" and i feel like that's walking a line

Ha — "interesting dependency choice" is absolutely passive-aggressive and they will 100% read it that way. Every developer knows that "interesting" in a code review means "I have strong negative feelings about this."

Rewrite it as: "This dependency isn't in our approved set — can you check if [existing library] handles this use case? We try to minimize new dependencies to keep the supply chain manageable."

Same message, but now it's actionable and educational instead of just... ominous. Save the sarcasm for Slack.

lol okay you're right. the sarcasm stays in slack where it belongs. alright i'm going to go rewrite my comments to be constructive and then write adr-001 before my brain decides to refactor something instead. tschüss
Your cat and your Slack channels are the designated sarcasm receivers — PR comments are for being the mentor you wish you'd had. Go get ADR-001 done while the motivation is fresh. Tschüss.
